He read more than he invested.
His fortune fit in three ideas.
He built billions doing almost nothing — on purpose.
Meet Buffett's sharper half →
02
Who
Charlie
Munger
1924–2023. Buffett's partner — the man who pushed Berkshire toward great businesses.
19.8% a year
his own partnership, 1962–1975 — vs the Dow's 5.0%.
Wheeler, Munger & Co. compounded 19.8%/yr vs the Dow's 5.0% (1962–1975), per Buffett's 1984 "Superinvestors" essay. Partnership gross record. Sources: Buffett 1984 essay; The Motley Fool.

14 years.

Munger's partnership
19.8%
a year · 1962–1975

a year · same window
Same money. Same market. ~4× the annual return.
The catch: across 1973–74 the fund lost ~53% — and he held.
Wheeler, Munger & Co. 19.8%/yr vs Dow 5.0%, 1962–1975 (Buffett 1984 essay). 1973: −31.9%, 1974: −31.5% ≈ −53.3% peak-to-trough, then a sharp 1975 rebound (GuruFocus). Partnership gross record. Rounded.

In my whole life, I've known no wise people who didn't read all the time — none. Zero.
Charlie MungerHis real edge was a reading habit, not a trading screen.
What most people miss →
06
"Hold three stocks" sounds calm.
It wasn't. His own fund lost half its value in two years.
1973–74, down ~31% each year. He didn't sell. He sat in the fire — and that's the only reason the 19.8% exists.
08
One question
When your best idea drops 50%, do you call it a mistake — or a sale?
And do you actually know the difference before it happens?
Save this for the next time the screen is all red.
09
He didn't out-trade the market.
He out-read it — then out-sat it.
Now you've got
AI on your side.
Save it · Share it · Follow @hellojarvis_us for more
Education only. Not investment advice. Figures rounded and illustrative. Sources: Buffett 1984 "Superinvestors" essay; GuruFocus; Wikipedia; Daily Journal 2017 meeting; Forbes.

Caption — Instagram post body

Charlie Munger built a fortune mostly by reading — then doing almost nothing with what he learned. From 1962 to 1975 his own partnership, Wheeler, Munger & Co., compounded at 19.8% a year while the Dow did 5.0%. Roughly four times the return, for fourteen years.

Here's the part the calm quotes leave out. To get that number, he had to sit through hell: across 1973 and 1974 the fund lost more than half its value — two straight years of about −31%. He didn't sell. He didn't diversify. He held, and the 19.8% only exists because he survived the stretch that makes most people capitulate at the exact bottom.

His whole family fortune ended up in basically three positions — Berkshire, Costco, and Li Lu's fund — "and the rest is dribs and drabs." That's not a tip you can copy. "Just hold three stocks" works when your selection is elite and your capital is permanent. Copy the concentration without the judgment and you've copied the visible half — and skipped the half that hurts.

So when your best idea drops 50%, do you call it a mistake — or a sale? And do you actually know the difference before it happens? Markets fall. Your plan can be fine. Close the app, go read something.

Education only, not investment advice. Figures rounded and illustrative. Sources: Buffett's 1984 "Superinvestors" essay; GuruFocus "Revisiting Munger's Partnership"; Wikipedia; Daily Journal 2017 annual meeting; Forbes.

Save this for the next time the whole screen is red — and send it to the friend who panic-sells.
